section.title: The Conquest of Ai
section.sectionRationale: With Achan's sin removed, God renews His word to Joshua and gives Ai into Israel's hand. This time the victory comes through an ambush God Himself directs, with Joshua holding out his battle lance until the city is utterly taken. It is one movement, the conquest of Ai, which shows God restoring His defeated people, giving them fresh assurance, and winning the victory that sin had forfeited, once the sin was dealt with.
section.thesis: The same God who withdrew when sin was hidden now says do not be afraid and delivers Ai, teaching that restoration follows repentance, that God graciously renews His promise to a people who had failed, and that the victory is His, signaled by Joshua's outstretched lance from first to last.
section.frameNote: Four beats: God's renewed command and the ambush plan, verses 1 to 9; the armies positioned and the feigned retreat, verses 10 to 17; Joshua's outstretched lance and the taking of the city, verses 18 to 23; and the destruction of Ai and its king, verses 24 to 29.

units.label: God's renewed command and the ambush plan
units.note: do not be afraid; I have delivered Ai into your hand

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Joshua, “Do not be {{yare|afraid}} or discouraged. Take the whole army with you, and go up and attack Ai. See, I have delivered into your hand the king of Ai, his people, his city, and his land.
And you shall do to Ai and its king as you did to Jericho and its king, except that you may carry off their plunder and livestock for yourselves. Set up an ambush behind the city.”
So Joshua and the whole army set out to attack Ai. Joshua chose 30,000 mighty men of valor and sent them out at night
with these orders: “Pay attention. You are to lie in ambush behind the city, not too far from it. All of you must be ready.
Then I and all the troops with me will advance on the city. When they come out against us as they did the first time, we will flee from them.
They will pursue us until we have drawn them away from the city, for they will say, ‘The Israelites are running away from us as they did before.’ So as we flee from them,
you are to rise from the ambush and seize the city, for the LORD your God will deliver it into your hand.
And when you have taken the city, set it on fire. Do as the LORD has commanded! See, I have given you orders.”
So Joshua sent them out, and they went to the place of ambush and lay in wait between Bethel and Ai, to the west of Ai. But Joshua spent that night among the people.

units.label: The armies positioned and the feigned retreat
units.note: Joshua let himself be beaten back to draw them out

verses.text: Joshua got up early the next morning and mobilized his men, and he and the elders of Israel marched before them up to Ai.
Then all the troops who were with him marched up and approached the city. They arrived in front of Ai and camped to the north of it, with the valley between them and the city.
Now Joshua had taken about five thousand men and set up an ambush between Bethel and Ai, to the west of the city.
So the forces were stationed with the main camp to the north of the city and the rear guard to the west of the city. And that night Joshua went into the valley.

When the king of Ai saw the Israelites, he hurried out early in the morning with the men of the city to engage them in battle at an appointed place overlooking the Arabah. But he did not know that an ambush had been set up against him behind the city.
Joshua and all Israel let themselves be beaten back before them, and they fled toward the wilderness.
Then all the men of Ai were summoned to pursue them, and they followed Joshua and were drawn away from the city.
Not a man was left in Ai or Bethel who did not go out after Israel, leaving the city wide open while they pursued Israel.

units.label: Joshua's outstretched lance and the taking of the city
units.note: into your hand I will deliver the city

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Joshua, “Hold out your battle {{kidon|lance}} toward Ai, for into your hand I will deliver the city.” So Joshua held out his battle lance toward Ai,
and as soon as he did so, the men in ambush rose quickly from their position. They rushed forward, entered the city, captured it, and immediately set it on fire.

When the men of Ai turned and looked back, the smoke of the city was rising into the sky. They could not escape in any direction, and the troops who had fled to the wilderness now turned against their pursuers.
When Joshua and all Israel saw that the men in ambush had captured the city and that smoke was rising from it, they turned around and struck down the men of Ai.

Meanwhile, those in the ambush came out of the city against them, and the men of Ai were trapped between the Israelite forces on both sides. So Israel struck them down until no survivor or fugitive remained.
But they took the king of Ai alive and brought him to Joshua.

units.label: The destruction of Ai and its king
units.note: Joshua made Ai a permanent heap of ruins

verses.text: When Israel had finished killing all the men of Ai who had pursued them into the field and wilderness, and when every last one of them had fallen by the sword, all the Israelites returned to Ai and put it to the sword as well.
A total of twelve thousand men and women fell that day, all the people of Ai.
Joshua did not draw back the hand that held his battle lance until he had {{cherem|devoted to destruction}} all who lived in Ai.
Israel took for themselves only the cattle and plunder of that city, as the LORD had commanded Joshua.
So Joshua burned Ai and made it a permanent heap of ruins, a desolation to this day.

He hung the king of Ai on a tree until evening, and at sunset Joshua commanded that they take down the body from the tree and throw it down at the entrance of the city gate. And over it they raised a large pile of rocks, which remains to this day.

yare.gloss: To fear, be afraid. After the shame of defeat, God's first word to Joshua is do not fear, the same reassurance He gave at the start, Joshua 1:9. Fear had gripped Israel when their hearts melted like water at Ai; now, with sin removed, God restores courage by pointing not to Israel's strength but to His own promise, I have delivered Ai into your hand.

kidon.gloss: A javelin, short spear, or battle lance. At God's word Joshua stretches out his kidon toward Ai and does not draw it back until the city is taken, recalling Moses' outstretched staff at the Red Sea and his raised hands at Amalek, Exodus 17:11. The lifted lance is the visible sign that the victory flows from God through His appointed leader, not from the soldiers' skill alone.

cherem.gloss: That which is devoted irrevocably to the LORD, here by destruction. Ai is placed under the cherem like Jericho, though this time God permits Israel to keep the plunder and livestock. The word ties the conquest of Ai to God's righteous judgment on Canaan and stands in pointed contrast to Achan, who stole what was under the ban; now the whole city is rightly given over to God.

basic.context: After Achan's sin was judged, God speaks again to Joshua with a word of grace: Do not be afraid. He restores the promise Israel had forfeited and gives them Ai, the very city that had defeated them. This time God directs a clever ambush. Joshua leads the main force in a pretended retreat to lure the men of Ai out of the city; then, at God's command, he holds out his battle lance, the signal for the hidden troops to storm the empty city and set it ablaze. Trapped between two Israelite forces, the men of Ai are destroyed, and the city is left a heap of ruins. God even allows Israel to keep the plunder this time. The question underneath is: what does God do with a people who have failed Him but turned back?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and it functions as the counterpart to chapter 7: where sin brought defeat, dealt-with sin opens the way to victory. The battle tactics are descriptive, not a manual, and the herem again raises the honest question of the conquest, which is God's judgment on Canaan. What endures is the pattern of restoration: God renews His promise and His presence to a repentant people, and He wins the victory that their sin had forfeited.
basic.originalLanguages: God's first word to the defeated is do not yare, do not fear, restoring courage by His promise. Joshua stretches out his kidon, his battle lance, and does not draw it back until the city falls, echoing Moses' outstretched hands. And Ai is placed under the cherem, devoted to the LORD, in righteous judgment.
basic.typology: Joshua's outstretched lance held up until victory recalls Moses' uplifted hands at Amalek and points to the sustained, victorious mediation of Christ. The king of Ai hung on a tree and taken down by evening connects to the law of the cursed one on a tree, which the New Testament applies to Christ, who became a curse for us.
basic.christ: The king of Ai was hung on a tree as a cursed enemy, and Deuteronomy says anyone hung on a tree is under God's curse; Paul says Christ became a curse for us by hanging on the tree, taking the curse we deserved. And the restoration of defeated Israel to victory pictures the grace by which Christ restores His failing people and gives them triumph they had forfeited.
basic.god: God is gracious to the fallen, speaking do not fear to a people who had just failed, and restoring the promise they had lost. He is sovereign over the battle, directing the ambush and giving the city. And He remains the holy Judge, bringing Ai under the ban even as He shows mercy to His own people. Grace and justice stand together.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 17 shows Moses' raised hands bringing victory over Amalek. Deuteronomy 21 gives the law of the one hung on a tree, taken down by evening. Galatians 3 says Christ became a curse for us on the tree. And 1 John 1 assures that God forgives and cleanses those who confess.
basic.oneStory: God restores His defeated people, giving them the victory at Ai that sin had cost them, once the sin was removed. It shows the grace of a God who says do not fear to those who have failed. The king hung on a tree and the leader's outstretched lance point forward to Christ, who became a curse for His people and secures their victory.

context.literary: Chapter 8 answers chapter 7 point for point: the defeat at Ai is reversed once Achan's sin is gone. God's opening do not be afraid echoes His charge in chapter 1 and signals restoration. The narrative is a carefully told battle account with an ambush, a feigned retreat, and a decisive signal, Joshua's outstretched lance, which frames the victory, held out in verse 18 and not drawn back until verse 26. The chapter closes the Ai episode and flows into the covenant renewal at Mount Ebal, verses 30 to 35, treated as the next movement.
context.historical: Ai lay in the hill country near Bethel; the two towns were close enough that Bethel's men joined the pursuit. An ambush and a feigned flight were recognized tactics, but the text stresses that the LORD directed and delivered. Devoting a city to destruction under the herem was God's judgment on Canaanite sin; here, unlike Jericho, Israel is permitted the plunder, showing Jericho's total ban was a special firstfruits case. Hanging an enemy king on a tree displayed defeat, and burying him under a heap of stones at the gate left a lasting monument, as with Achan.
context.canonical: The conquest of Ai illustrates the biblical rhythm of sin, judgment, repentance, and restoration, and it shows God's readiness to renew His promise to a chastened people. Joshua's outstretched lance ties the scene to Moses at Amalek, reinforcing Joshua as Moses' true successor. Most significantly, the king of Ai hung on a tree and taken down by evening connects to Deuteronomy 21:22-23, the law Paul cites in Galatians 3:13 to explain that Christ became a curse for us by hanging on the tree, so that even this grim detail points toward the cross.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God restoring His defeated people once sin was removed, renewing His promise and presence, directing the victory at Ai, and confirming that the triumph comes from the LORD through His appointed leader, while executing righteous judgment on the Canaanite city and its king.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The military tactics and the herem belong to a specific moment in redemptive history and are not prescriptions for later warfare; the church's warfare is spiritual. What endures is the theology: God graciously restores the repentant, He renews courage by His promise rather than by human strength, and He is both the merciful Savior of His people and the just Judge of entrenched sin.

debates.question: Why was Israel allowed to keep the plunder of Ai when Jericho's spoil had been wholly banned?

views.label: Jericho was the firstfruits, wholly God's
views.case: As the first city of the land, Jericho was consecrated entirely to the LORD like a firstfruits offering, so all its spoil was His; Ai, coming after, falls under the more usual pattern in which God grants the plunder to His people, showing that Jericho's total ban was a special, one-time consecration, not the norm.

views.label: A pointed contrast with Achan's sin
views.case: The permission to take Ai's plunder, by the LORD's explicit command, throws Achan's theft into sharp relief: what he grasped unlawfully at Jericho, God freely gives at Ai; the timing underscores that the problem was never the plunder itself but seizing what God had reserved for Himself.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that obedience to God's specific word is the point: the same items forbidden at Jericho are granted at Ai, so blessing comes not from grabbing but from receiving what God gives on His terms.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words trace the movement from fear to victory. God's very first word to the defeated Joshua is do not yare, do not be afraid, the same reassurance that opened the book, Joshua 1:9; the fear that had melted Israel's heart at Ai is answered not by a bigger army but by God's promise, I have delivered Ai into your hand. Then, at the climax, Joshua stretches out his kidon, his battle lance, toward the city and does not draw back his hand until the work is done, a deliberate echo of Moses lifting his staff over the sea and holding up his hands at Amalek so that Israel prevailed, Exodus 17:11; the lifted lance makes the leader the visible channel of a victory that flows from God. Finally, Ai is placed under the cherem, devoted to the LORD by destruction, the same righteous judgment as Jericho, though this time God grants the plunder, quietly rebuking Achan, who had stolen what God had reserved. Fear removed, lance uplifted, city devoted: the words show God restoring, leading, and judging.

typology.type: Joshua's lance held out until the victory is complete
typology.fulfillment: Moses' uplifted hands at Amalek and, beyond both, Christ's unfailing, victorious intercession for His people, Exodus 17:11-12 and Hebrews 7:25.

typology.type: The king of Ai hung on a tree and taken down by evening
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who became a curse for us by hanging on the tree, taken down before nightfall, Deuteronomy 21:22-23 and Galatians 3:13.

typology.type: Defeated Israel restored to victory after repentance
typology.fulfillment: Christ restoring His failing people and giving them the triumph they had forfeited, 1 John 1:9 and 2 Corinthians 2:14.

study.christ: Two threads of this chapter run straight to Christ. First, Joshua stretches out his battle lance toward Ai and holds it out, hand unwavering, until the last enemy has fallen, just as Moses held up his hands at Amalek so that Israel prevailed as long as his arms were raised. Both point to the Lord Jesus, whose intercession for His people never fails and never falters, so that He is able to save completely those who draw near to God through Him, since He always lives to intercede, Hebrews 7:25. Second, the king of Ai is hung on a tree as a cursed and defeated enemy, then taken down by evening in obedience to the law that says, cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree, Deuteronomy 21:23. Paul takes that very law and says Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written, cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree, Galatians 3:13. Even this dark scene of judgment prepares for the cross, where the true King bore the curse we deserved.
study.god: The God of this chapter is gracious to the fallen and mighty in battle. To a people freshly humiliated by defeat and freshly sobered by judgment, His first word is not rebuke but reassurance: Do not be afraid. He restores the promise Israel had forfeited by sin and gives them the very city that had beaten them, proving that failure need not be final for those who deal honestly with their sin. He is sovereign over the whole battle, designing the ambush, timing the signal, and delivering the city into their hand, so the victory is unmistakably His. And He remains the holy Judge, bringing Ai and its king under righteous judgment even as He shows mercy to His own. In this God we see grace and justice held perfectly together, the same God who will one day bear the curse Himself to save the guilty.

crossReferences.note: God's do not be afraid restores to the defeated people the same courage-giving promise that opened the book.
Israel prevails as long as Moses holds up his hands, the pattern behind Joshua's outstretched lance.
The law of the one hung on a tree, cursed by God and taken down by evening, obeyed with the king of Ai.
Christ became a curse for us by hanging on the tree, redeeming us from the curse of the law.
If we confess our sins, God is faithful to forgive and cleanse, the grace that restored Israel after Achan.

study.oneStory: Once the sin of Achan was removed, God restored His defeated people and gave them the victory at Ai that their sin had cost them, opening with the tender word do not be afraid. It is a picture of grace: God renews His promise and presence to a chastened people and wins the battle for them. The leader's outstretched lance and the cursed king hung on a tree point forward to Christ, whose intercession never fails and who became a curse for His people, securing the victory they could never have won on their own.

quiz.q: What was God's first word to Joshua before the second attack on Ai?

options.text: Do not be afraid or discouraged, for He had delivered Ai into their hand.
options.note: Verse 1 opens with reassurance and a renewed promise after the defeat.

options.text: A stern rebuke for Israel's earlier failure.
options.note: The failure had been dealt with in chapter 7; God's word here is reassurance, not rebuke.

options.text: A command to abandon Ai and move on to another city.
options.note: God did not tell them to abandon Ai; He gave it into their hand.

options.text: A warning that He would no longer help them.
options.note: The opposite: God promised to deliver the city into their hand.

quiz.q: How did Israel take the city of Ai?

options.text: By a God-directed ambush, luring the men of Ai out with a feigned retreat, then seizing the empty city.
options.note: Verses 3 to 22 describe the ambush and pretended flight that God directed.

options.text: By marching around it for seven days as at Jericho.
options.note: That was the method at Jericho; Ai was taken by ambush, not by marching.

options.text: By laying a long siege until the city surrendered.
options.note: There was no drawn-out siege; the city fell in a single day through the ambush.

options.text: By negotiating a peaceful surrender with its king.
options.note: There was no negotiation; the city was taken by force and destroyed.

quiz.q: What was the significance of Joshua holding out his battle lance?

options.text: It was the signal for the ambush and the sign that God was giving the victory through His appointed leader.
options.note: Verses 18 and 26 tie the outstretched lance to God's promise and Israel's triumph, echoing Moses.

options.text: It was merely a way to point out the direction of attack.
options.note: It did signal the ambush, but its deeper meaning was that the victory came from God, as with Moses' raised hands.

options.text: It was a magic weapon that destroyed the city on its own.
options.note: The lance was not magical; it was a sign of God-given victory, not the cause in itself.

options.text: It showed that Joshua fought the whole battle single-handedly.
options.note: The whole army fought; the lance signaled and symbolized God's deliverance, not solo combat.

quiz.q: How did the conquest of Ai differ from Jericho regarding the plunder?

options.text: At Ai God permitted Israel to keep the plunder and livestock, unlike the total ban at Jericho.
options.note: Verses 2 and 27 show God granting the plunder of Ai, a pointed contrast with Achan's theft at Jericho.

options.text: At Ai everything was banned even more strictly than at Jericho.
options.note: The reverse is true: Ai's plunder was permitted, whereas Jericho's had been wholly banned.

options.text: At Ai the plunder was given away to the eastern tribes.
options.note: The plunder was kept by Israel generally, as God commanded, not given specifically to the eastern tribes.

options.text: At Ai there was no plunder to be found.
options.note: There was plunder, and God specifically allowed Israel to keep it.

quiz.q: How does the king of Ai hung on a tree point to Christ?

options.text: The one hung on a tree was under God's curse, and Paul says Christ became a curse for us on the tree.
options.note: Deuteronomy 21:23 and Galatians 3:13 connect the cursed one on the tree to Christ's cross.

options.text: It shows that God delights in the death of His enemies for its own sake.
options.note: It displays judgment, but its deeper pointer is to Christ bearing the curse, not to delight in death.

options.text: It teaches that hanging bodies on trees should continue today.
options.note: The specific practice belonged to that setting; its lasting meaning points to Christ bearing the curse.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: Galatians 3:13 explicitly links the cursed one on the tree to Christ's cross.

questions: After a painful failure, God met Joshua with do not be afraid and restored the promise. How does it comfort you that failure need not be the end of the story for those who turn back to God?
Israel regained the very ground that had defeated them, but only after sin was removed. Is there an area of repeated defeat in your life where God may be calling you to deal with hidden sin first?
The victory came from God, signaled by Joshua's outstretched lance. Where are you tempted to credit your successes to your own skill rather than to God's hand?
Joshua held out his lance and did not draw it back until the work was done, a picture of Christ's unfailing intercession. How does knowing Jesus never stops praying for you strengthen you today?
The king of Ai bore the curse of the tree, pointing to Christ who became a curse for us. How does the cross, where Jesus took the curse you deserved, move your gratitude and worship?

translationNotes.note: Battle lance renders a word the BSB footnote also gives as javelin, appearing twice in the verse and again in verse 26; the outstretched lance recalls Moses at Amalek.
The BSB sets off all the people of Ai with an em dash; this has been rendered as a comma here, with no change to the wording.
Devoted to destruction renders a form of the Hebrew cherem, as the BSB footnote notes; Ai came under the same ban as Jericho.
Hung on a tree renders a phrase the BSB footnote also gives as impaled on a pole; taking the body down by evening obeys Deuteronomy 21:22-23.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 8:1-29. Prose narrative. The em dash in verse 25 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Numbers such as 30,000 and 12,000 are retained as in the BSB. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
